text: When I did my review of Ballerina a few
days ago, the most obvious point of
comparison was Furiosa, and it's not
hard to see why. Both movies were
femaleled spin-offs from established
male action franchises. Both starred
pretty likable actresses playing
well-rounded protagonists. Both came
away with solid audience scores and
positive critical reviews. There was no
backlash, no negativity around them, no
controversies or media firestorms, no
actors shooting their mouths off. It's
Hollywood, baby. And yet both of them
flopped at the box office. The question
is why? Well, this is where things get a
bit contentious. So, pay attention, dear
viewer, because we're taking a deep dive
into the murky world of film franchises.
One of the fundamental problems that
both of these films struggled with is
that it's always tricky to launch a
spin-off from a franchise that's so
heavily based around its central
character. Franchises like Star Wars or
Marvel or Star Trek are bigger than any
one character or story, and so they lend
themselves much more easily to spin-offs
and side projects. It's more about the
world than the people in it.
Character-based franchises are a
completely different story, though,
because they are the selling point. In
this case, both John Wick and Mad Max
soon became bigger and more memorable
than the actual movies they were part
of. Most people would probably struggle
to remember the plots for John Wick 2
and three, but man, they remember Keanu
Reeves was cool, and that's what
they really gravitated towards. That
right there is what kept them coming
back for the next installment. MadMax
Fury Road kind of struggled with this
problem because Mel Gibson had aged out
of the role while the movies were on
hiatus, forcing the studio to bring in a
younger Tom Hardy to replace him. They
more or less got away with that though.
Partly because the awesome action scenes
compensated for it. But also because
there was still some kind of continuity
there. Even if the actor might have
changed, it was still the same character
they'd come to know and love over the
years. Both Furiosa and Eve from
Ballerina didn't have the benefit of all
that fan goodwill and attachment. They
had to earn it like they were starting
from scratch. They weren't piggybacking
off the success of their predecessors.
They had to stand on their own merits
and earn it for themselves.
Unfortunately, their studios didn't seem
to understand this and budgeted both
movies like they were mainline sequels.
To put this into context, the original
John Wick brought in $86 million
worldwide, which isn't exactly blowing
the lid off the global box office, but
it didn't have to. It only cost $20
million to make, and so that $86 million
generated a nice little profit for the
studio. Ballerina, on the other hand, is
projected to make about a h 100red
million worldwide, which would have been
great news if the studio had the sense
to budget it like the first John Wick,
but they didn't. It cost 90 million to
make, more than four times as much as
the first movie, and so it needs to make
four times more to turn a profit. And
clearly, it's not going to make it. It's
the same story with Furiosa. That film
brought in 178 million worldwide, which
would have been great news if it had
cost 40 or 50 million, but it didn't. It
cost 168 million. That's insane for a
movie with an unproven character from a
franchise that always specialized in
low-budget action thrillers. My point
here is that spin-offs generally don't
generate as much interest or as much
revenue as the original movies. And if
both studios had been realistic about
what these films could actually achieve
and adjusted their expectations and
budgets accordingly, they might have
ended up turning a solid profit instead
of being depressing flops. Now, that's
the business side of the equation, but
there's a much bigger problem that's
brought down both of these films. A
cultural backlash that's been building
up for years now and has finally
exploded harder than me after a
particularly strenuous session with
Tatiana. The fact is, audiences today
are getting a bit sick of girl
boss movies because they've had nothing
but girl boss movies shoved down their
throats for the past decade or so. And
in particular, they're sick of seeing
their favorite male-led franchises,
getting taken over by unlikable,
arrogant, abrasive, and infallible
feminist power fantasies, usually
written by talentless hacks and
embittered activists who they're
commanded to like and respect simply
because they've been crowbarred in to
replace someone far more popular. Fan
bases don't work like that. The big pop
culture franchises are the most obvious
and high-profile examples of this, but
the truth is that it's spread to almost
all facets of entertainment. Like, how
many times have you been told that some
old movie or TV show is getting a
revival, updated for modern audiences,
of course, usually followed by sneering
think pieces, decrying the original as a
dated relic of a less enlightened time,
and fawning interviews with pretentious
actors up on their soap boxes about how
excited they are to bring a modern
dimension to their formerly outdated
characters. How many times have you seen
your favorite male heroes brought out of
cold storage just so they can be mocked,
belittled, outperformed, and ultimately
replaced by their idealized, overpowered
female superiors. It's all happened so
many times now that even the most
easygoing, culturally neutral normies
are just kind of done with it.
It's exhausting and demoralizing, and
most people just can't handle it
anymore. And at worst, a good chunk of
audiences are actively rebelling against
it. And what's the end result of all
this? Well, in blunt terms, the female
action star is dead now.
Audiences have been burned so many times
now that anytime a new femaleled movie
shows up, they basically roll their eyes
and say, "Ah, here we go,
another girl boss." They're unwilling to
give any femaleled action movies a
chance now to the point where actual
good and respectful examples like
Furiosa and Ballerina all get lumped
together and flush down the same
cultural toilet bowl as the watery shite
excreted by Disney, Marvel, and Amazon.
And when I think about it, that really
is a shame because believe it or not,
there was once a time when we really did
have it all. go back as recently as the
2000s or even the early 2010s and we had
a whole bunch of established femaleled
franchises like Resident Evil,
Underworld, Tomb Raider, Kill Bill, and
The Hunger Games. And you know what?
Nobody complained about them
then. There were no boycots or
backlashes or cultural warfare raging
around them. Nobody cared because they
existed alongside male-led franchises
instead of trying to replace them. There
was enough room for everyone and there
still is today. The problem is that the
well has been poisoned to such an extent
that it's treated more like a battle to
the death that only one side can walk
away from instead of a fun and vibrant
genre that encourages new ideas and
opportunities. One of the fundamental
rules of the universe is that every
action has a reaction. And if your
action is to spend years on
male heroes just to elevate lowquality
female replacements, then guess what the
reaction is? The audiences will stop
watching what you make, whether it's
good or bads. The female action star is
dead because the girl boss killed it.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
